title: Frame loom warp stripe sampler
subtitle: A beginner frame loom project using multi-colour warp stripes and a plain weft
excerpt: A warp stripe sampler uses alternating colours of yarn set in the warp before weaving begins. Because the stripe pattern is fixed in the warp, it remains unchanged throughout weaving and the structure is simple enough to learn on. A good first project for anyone who has not woven before.
sourceNotes: Warp stripe weaving is a fundamental technique consistent across all frame loom and introductory weaving instruction. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads held under tension on the loom, running lengthways through the finished cloth. Warp threads are wound onto the loom before weaving begins. In warp stripe weaving, alternating colours of warp thread create vertical stripes in the finished cloth.

The thread woven over and under the warp threads horizontally to build up the cloth. The weft travels across the full width of the cloth in each row and is beaten down after each pass. In a warp stripe sampler, the weft is typically a single neutral colour that allows the warp stripes to show.

The side edges of a woven cloth where the weft turns back at the end of each row. Clean selvedges are formed by leaving a slight arc of weft in each row before beating; this allows the weft to follow the warp threads without drawing the edges inward.

content.text: stripe sampler is a good first weaving project. The pattern is set before weaving begins and requires no decisions during weaving. Alternating the warp colour in a planned sequence produces stripes that run the full length of the cloth; the
in a single neutral colour holds the warp threads together without interrupting the stripe pattern.

items.name: DK weight yarn, 2 to 4 stripe colours for the warp
DK weight yarn, 1 neutral colour for the weft
Frame loom, minimum 20 cm wide and 25 cm tall
Blunt darning needle or stick shuttle for weaving weft
Fork or comb for beating down weft rows

content.text: Step 1: Plan and wind the warp

Decide on the stripe sequence before you begin: for example, 4 threads of colour A, 4 threads of colour B, 2 threads of colour C, repeated across the width. Sketch the stripe sequence on paper and count the total threads needed for your loom width. Wind the warp onto the loom by looping each thread over the top peg and under the bottom peg, alternating in the correct colour order across the full width. Keep the tension even: each warp thread should feel equally taut when pressed with a finger.
Thread the neutral weft yarn onto a blunt darning needle. Beginning at the bottom of the warp, weave over one thread and under the next across the full width. At the

, leave a slight arc of weft before turning back: do not pull the yarn tight against the edge warp thread. Return across the row with the opposite over-under sequence. After every two rows, press the weft down firmly with a fork or comb. Consistent beating keeps the rows evenly packed and the cloth at the same density throughout.
Step 3: Finish and remove from loom

When the weaving reaches the top of the loom, cut the warp threads and tie each pair in an overhand knot close to the cloth edge to hold the weft in place. The resulting fringe at each end can be left, trimmed to an even length, or folded to the back of the cloth and stitched down. Hand wash the finished sampler in warm water and press under a damp cloth.

items.problem: The cloth is narrowing as weaving progresses and the edges curve inward
items.cause: The weft is being pulled tight at the selvedge rather than leaving a weft arc
items.fix: After each pass, use a finger to push a small arc of weft at each edge before beating. The weft needs extra length to wrap around the outer warp threads without pulling them inward.

items.problem: The weft rows are packed unevenly with thick and thin sections
items.cause: The beating pressure was inconsistent between rows
items.fix: Use the fork or comb with the same force on every row. After every 5 to 6 rows, check that the row density is even by measuring the woven length at the centre and at both edges: all three should match.
